findings.topic: MCP host/client/server architecture and isolation model
findings.summary: MCP (spec rev 2025-06-18) is a 'client-host-server architecture where each host can run multiple client instances ... Built on JSON-RPC, MCP provides a stateful session protocol focused on context exchange and sampling coordination.' The host 'creates and manages multiple client instances, controls client connection permissions and lifecycle, enforces security policies and consent requirements, handles user authorization decisions, coordinates AI/LLM integration and sampling.' Each client maintains exactly one stateful session per server (1:1 client:server). Hard isolation principle: 'Servers should not be able to read the whole conversation, nor see into other servers' — servers receive only necessary context, full conversation history stays with the host, cross-server interactions are controlled by the host. Capability negotiation is mandatory: servers declare resources/tools/prompts/subscriptions, clients declare sampling/notification handling, and 'both parties must respect declared capabilities throughout the session.'
findings.designImplication: The MCP host role IS the kernel role in an agent-native OS: connection-permission control, consent enforcement, context aggregation, and cross-server isolation are exactly kernel responsibilities. Model each tool/context provider connection as a kernel object (like a file descriptor) with a 1:1 session, kernel-enforced 'server cannot see the whole conversation' isolation (analogous to address-space isolation), and capability declaration recorded at handshake time so the kernel can reject undeclared operations without involving the agent's LLM.

findings.topic: MCP wire layer: JSON-RPC 2.0 message types, lifecycle, and _meta extensibility
findings.summary: All MCP messages MUST follow JSON-RPC 2.0. Three message kinds: Requests (id MUST be string|int, MUST NOT be null, MUST NOT be reused within a session), Responses (exactly one of result|error; integer error codes), and Notifications (one-way, MUST NOT include an id). Mandatory components are only 'base protocol and lifecycle management'; everything else (resources, prompts, tools, sampling, roots, utilities like logging/completion) is optional and negotiated. Lifecycle is three-phase (per survey 2505.02279 §4.5): Initialization (version negotiation to highest mutually supported, capability exchange, client sends notifications/initialized), Operation (calls per negotiated capabilities, per-request timeouts with cancellation notifications), Shutdown (transport close, resource cleanup). A reserved '_meta' property with a prefixed key-name grammar allows attaching protocol-level metadata without schema changes. Auth is transport-dependent: HTTP transports SHOULD use the OAuth-based authorization framework; stdio transports SHOULD instead 'retrieve credentials from the environment.'
findings.designImplication: The kernel IPC layer needs exactly three primitives: correlated request/response (with kernel-enforced unique-id-per-session semantics), one-way notification, and cancellation — plus a versioned capability handshake. Adopt the 'minimal mandatory core + negotiated optional features' pattern for the kernel ABI itself so subsystems can evolve independently. The stdio-vs-HTTP auth split is instructive: local kernel IPC should inherit credentials from the kernel principal (no tokens on the local path), while remote transports carry explicit OAuth-class auth in userspace.

findings.topic: MCP primitives mapped to a control-plane taxonomy (tools/resources/prompts/sampling/roots/elicitation)
findings.summary: MCP server primitives: Tools, Resources, Prompts; client primitives: Sampling, Roots ('root directory lists provided by clients'), Elicitation. Survey 2505.02279 §4.4 gives the control taxonomy: 'Tools are model-controlled... Resources are application-controlled... Prompts are user-controlled templates... Sampling is server-controlled and allows the MCP Server to delegate the task of generating LLM completions to the client.' Sampling (sampling/createMessage) lets a server request a generation 'with no server API keys necessary'; model selection is abstracted via modelPreferences: three normalized 0-1 priorities (costPriority, speedPriority, intelligencePriority) plus advisory substring-matching hints ('clients MAY map hints to equivalent models from different providers', e.g. mapping a claude-3-sonnet hint to gemini-1.5-pro). Elicitation (elicitation/create) requests structured human input validated by a deliberately restricted JSON Schema subset ('flat objects with primitive properties only' — string/number/boolean/enum, no nesting/arrays) with a three-action result model: accept / decline / cancel; 'Servers MUST NOT request sensitive information through elicitation.'
findings.designImplication: These six primitives are a ready-made syscall taxonomy for an agent-native kernel, each with a distinct authority: tool-call (model authority), resource-read (app authority), prompt-template (user authority), inference (delegated authority), filesystem-scope/roots (sandbox boundary), human-input (consent channel). Sampling is the blueprint for the OS's LLM-agnostic inference service: a kernel 'inference syscall' that takes capability-priority vectors and advisory hints, with the kernel/router — not the caller — binding to a concrete backend (Claude API, vLLM, llama.cpp). Elicitation's restricted-schema + accept/decline/cancel design should be the kernel's default human-in-the-loop primitive because its bounded schema is cheap to validate in-kernel.

findings.topic: MCP transports: stdio pipes and Streamable HTTP with resumable sessions
findings.summary: Two standard transports; 'Clients SHOULD support stdio whenever possible.' stdio: client launches server as a subprocess; newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout; stderr reserved for logging; stdout MUST carry nothing but valid MCP messages. Streamable HTTP: single MCP endpoint supporting POST (every client JSON-RPC message is a new POST) and GET (server-initiated SSE stream); server replies application/json or opens an SSE stream; sessions via Mcp-Session-Id header ('globally unique and cryptographically secure'), HTTP 404 forces re-initialization, DELETE terminates; resumability via per-stream SSE event IDs + Last-Event-ID replay ('the server MUST NOT replay messages that would have been delivered on a different stream'); MCP-Protocol-Version header on every request (fallback assumption: 2025-03-26). Security: 'Servers MUST validate the Origin header... to prevent DNS rebinding attacks', bind to 127.0.0.1 locally. Custom transports are explicitly allowed if they 'preserve the JSON-RPC message format and lifecycle requirements.'
findings.designImplication: MCP's transport-agnosticism is a gift to a greenfield kernel: keep the MCP message/lifecycle layer but replace both transports with a native kernel IPC channel (shared-memory ring or pipe-pair) as the default — stdio's subprocess model already is the unix pipe model, so MCP servers become OS-native processes with zero adaptation. Kernel must own per-stream sequence numbers and replay buffers (the Last-Event-ID pattern) so agent sessions survive agent restarts/migrations — this is the hook for the OS's persistent-session guarantee. Remote Streamable HTTP belongs in a userspace network daemon that bridges to the kernel channel.

findings.topic: MCP trajectory (2025-11-25 revision): durable tasks, sampling with tools, standardized auth discovery
findings.summary: The 2025-11-25 spec revision adds: 'experimental support for tasks to enable tracking durable requests with polling and deferred result retrieval (SEP-1686)'; 'tool calling support to sampling via tools and toolChoice parameters (SEP-1577)'; URL-mode elicitation (SEP-1036); richer elicitation enums (SEP-1330); JSON Schema 2020-12 as the default schema dialect (SEP-1613); OpenID Connect Discovery 1.0 for authorization-server discovery; OAuth Client ID Metadata Documents as recommended client registration (SEP-991); incremental scope consent via WWW-Authenticate (SEP-835); polling SSE streams where 'servers may disconnect at will' (SEP-1699); input-validation errors reclassified as Tool Execution Errors 'to enable model self-correction' (SEP-1303); icons metadata for tools/resources/prompts; formal governance (SEP-932) and SDK tiering (SEP-1730).
findings.designImplication: MCP is converging toward what A2A already has (durable tasks) — strong evidence that a durable, pollable task object must be a first-class kernel object from day one, not a protocol afterthought. 'Tool calling inside sampling' means the kernel inference service must support nested tool dispatch (an inference syscall that can re-enter the tool layer) — plan re-entrancy into the kernel inference path. Returning validation errors to the model rather than failing the protocol (SEP-1303) is an agent-native error philosophy the kernel should adopt globally: errors are feedback for self-correction, so kernel error returns should be structured and model-readable by default.

findings.topic: A2A layered specification: canonical proto data model, abstract operations, three interchangeable bindings
findings.summary: A2A v1.0.0 (open standard under the Linux Foundation) is specified in three layers: (1) canonical data model — Task, Message, AgentCard, Part, Artifact, Extension — defined normatively in spec/a2a.proto ('the single authoritative normative definition'); (2) abstract operations independent of transport; (3) protocol bindings: JSON-RPC, gRPC, HTTP+JSON/REST, plus custom bindings. 11 operations in the method-mapping table: SendMessage (POST /message:send), SendStreamingMessage (POST /message:stream), GetTask (GET /tasks/{id}), ListTasks, CancelTask (POST /tasks/{id}:cancel), SubscribeToTask (POST /tasks/{id}:subscribe), 4 push-notification-config CRUD ops, GetExtendedAgentCard (GET /extendedAgentCard). Strict functional-equivalence rule: multi-protocol agents MUST provide 'identical functionality, consistent behavior, same error handling, equivalent authentication' across bindings; agents MUST declare all supported protocols in the AgentCard and clients MAY choose any. Version negotiation via A2A-Version header (Major.Minor, e.g. '1.0'); empty header interpreted as 0.3; unsupported version → VersionNotSupportedError. Guiding principle: 'Opaque Execution: Agents collaborate based on declared capabilities and exchanged information, without needing to share their internal thoughts, plans, or tool implementations.'
findings.designImplication: A2A's layering is the correct shape for the OS: define one canonical, machine-verified kernel ABI for inter-agent operations (ideally schema-generated like a2a.proto) and treat JSON-RPC/gRPC/REST as userspace binding shims, with functional equivalence as a conformance test the OS can run against any binding. 'Opaque execution' should be a kernel guarantee: an agent's working memory/plan state is kernel-protected private memory; only declared capabilities and exchanged messages cross the boundary — internal state sharing requires an explicit grant.

findings.topic: A2A task state machine: 9 states with terminal and human/credential-blocked interrupt states
findings.summary: A2A Tasks are 'the fundamental unit of work... stateful and progress through a defined lifecycle' with 9 enum states: TASK_STATE_UNSPECIFIED, SUBMITTED, WORKING, COMPLETED, FAILED, CANCELED, INPUT_REQUIRED, REJECTED, AUTH_REQUIRED. Four are terminal (COMPLETED, FAILED, CANCELED, REJECTED — terminal tasks reject further messages with UnsupportedOperationError); two are 'interrupted' states (INPUT_REQUIRED — agent needs user input; AUTH_REQUIRED — authentication needed to proceed). SendMessage supports blocking mode (wait until terminal OR interrupted state) and non-blocking (return_immediately: true, caller polls GetTask / subscribes / receives webhooks). Multiple concurrent subscribe streams per task are required: 'Events MUST be broadcast to all active streams for that task; each stream MUST receive the same events in the same order; closing one stream MUST NOT affect other active streams.' ListTasks uses cursor-based pagination (pageToken/nextPageToken) explicitly to avoid the deep-pagination problem. Update events are TaskStatusUpdateEvent and TaskArtifactUpdateEvent; outputs are Artifacts composed of Parts (text, file references, structured data).
findings.designImplication: This is a process-table design for agent work: the kernel scheduler should represent agent tasks with exactly this shape — including REJECTED (agent refusal as a first-class scheduler outcome, unique to agent-native systems) and the two blocked-states INPUT_REQUIRED/AUTH_REQUIRED, which generalize unix 'blocked on I/O' to 'blocked on human' and 'blocked on credential'. Kernel-level fan-out of ordered task events to N observers (the multi-stream rule) is the basis for multi-agent sessions where several agents watch one task. Artifacts as Parts-composed outputs suggest a kernel artifact store distinct from the message stream.

findings.topic: A2A Agent Card: signed capability manifest with gated extended view and tenant routing
findings.summary: AgentCard is 'a self-describing manifest for an agent' — required fields: name, description, supportedInterfaces (ordered array of AgentInterface, 'the first entry is preferred'), version, capabilities (streaming, pushNotifications, extensions, extendedAgentCard booleans), defaultInputModes/defaultOutputModes (media types), skills (array of AgentSkill); optional: securitySchemes/securityRequirements, provider, signatures ('array of AgentCardSignature — JSON Web Signatures computed for this AgentCard', RFC 7515 JWS). Capability gating is enforced by errors: if capabilities.pushNotifications is false, the 4 config ops MUST return PushNotificationNotSupportedError; streaming false → UnsupportedOperationError; GetExtendedAgentCard requires authentication via 'one of the schemes declared in the public AgentCard.securitySchemes' and returns a card that 'may contain additional details or skills not present in the public card.' AgentInterface carries an opaque 'tenant' routing field for 'routing requests to a specific agent or tenant when multiple agents are served behind a single A2A endpoint.' Extensions are URI-identified; a required extension that the client requests at an unsupported version MUST produce an error ('It MUST NOT fall back to a previous version of the extension automatically'). Discovery in the April/May-2025-era ecosystem was via the Agent Card 'served at /.well-known/agent.json' (survey §5.4) fetched by plain HTTP GET.
findings.designImplication: The Agent Card is the agent-native analog of a signed executable manifest: the OS should require every installed agent to ship a signed capability manifest, verify the JWS at load time (kernel or trusted loader), and then enforce declared capabilities mechanically — the A2A pattern of 'undeclared capability → typed error' becomes 'undeclared capability → kernel EPERM-equivalent'. Public-vs-extended card maps to two-tier introspection: anonymous capability summary vs authenticated full manifest. Card publication, well-known-URI serving, and registry indexing are userspace concerns (a discovery daemon), but manifest verification and capability enforcement are kernel concerns.

findings.topic: ACP (IBM/BeeAI, Linux Foundation): REST-native messaging, MIME-typed multipart, offline discovery, await/resume sessions
findings.summary: ACP is 'an open protocol for agent interoperability' using 'a standardized RESTful API' — explicitly contrasted with JSON-RPC: 'Unlike protocols requiring specialized communication methods (such as JSON-RPC), ACP leverages familiar HTTP conventions.' Messages are ordered lists of parts with 'explicit MIME content_type annotations and either embedded content or dereferenceable content_url values' — 'any mimetype works out of the box.' Async-first with sync supported; stateful and stateless patterns; streaming; long-running tasks; 'No SDK required (but available)' — usable from curl. Distinctive: 'Offline Discovery: Agents can be made discoverable even when inactive by embedding metadata directly into their distribution packages... in secure, disconnected, or scale-to-zero environments'; discovery also via registries, /.well-known/agent.yml manifests, or container labels (survey §6.1). Agents support 'await/resume for interactive use cases'; the ACP server is 'stateless by default' for load-balancer/Kubernetes compatibility; task states include created, in_progress, awaiting (survey §6.2). Governed 'as an open standard under the Linux Foundation alongside BeeAI (its reference implementation).' Launched by IBM March 2025 (survey §1).
findings.designImplication: Two ACP ideas belong in the OS by default: (1) offline discovery via package-embedded metadata — the OS's agent package format should carry the full agent manifest so the system catalog can enumerate and match dormant agents without spawning them (critical for scale-to-zero agent scheduling); (2) await/resume — kernel-level suspend of an agent session pending external input, which composes with the OS's persistent tiered memory (suspended session state lives in the memory subsystem, not in transport buffers). ACP's MIME-typed ordered parts are a good default payload container for the kernel message format because they make multimodality a content-type concern, not a protocol revision.

findings.topic: ANP: three-layer decentralized stack — DID identity, natural-language meta-protocol negotiation, JSON-LD description/discovery
findings.summary: ANP's white paper defines a three-layer architecture: (1) Identity and Secure Communication Layer — W3C DID with a custom did:wba (Web-Based Agent) method extending did:web; first HTTP request carries DID + signature, server fetches the DID document, verifies, returns a token for subsequent requests; ECDHE-based end-to-end encryption so 'intermediate nodes cannot decrypt the communication content' (enabling untrusted reverse proxies). (2) Meta-Protocol Layer — agents negotiate the communication protocol itself in natural language: request → negotiation rounds → 'each party generates and deploys protocol processing code based on the negotiated protocol' (AI code generation) → joint testing → formal communication; negotiation results are cached and shareable because per-communication negotiation 'will result in enormous cost consumption.' (3) Application Layer — Agent Description Protocol (ADP): JSON-LD documents using schema.org vocabulary (Agent, Service, Interface), bound to a DID; discovery via active mode (domain-level /.well-known/agent-descriptions returning a paginated CollectionPage of AD URLs) and passive mode (registration APIs of search-service agents that crawl and index). Security: a dedicated 'humanAuthorization' DID verification method — low-risk ops auto-authorized by agent keys, high-risk ops (fund transfers, privacy disclosure) 'must be explicitly authorized by human users' via biometric/password/HSM confirmation; multi-DID strategy (primary DID for long-term relationships, rotating least-privilege sub-DIDs per scenario); minimal-disclosure principle.
findings.designImplication: Clean kernel/userspace split inside one protocol: the identity layer is kernel material — agent identity as a kernel principal with key operations isolated (TEE/HSM-backed keyring service), and the humanAuthorization pattern should become a kernel-enforced consent gate on a tagged set of high-risk syscalls (two-keyring model: autonomous key vs human-gated key). The meta-protocol layer is emphatically userspace — LLM-driven, code-generating, latency-tolerant — but its negotiation-result cache is a natural client of the OS's persistent memory tiers and self-improvement service (negotiated protocol adapters are learned, stored, reused artifacts). Multi-DID rotation suggests kernel support for per-task derived sub-identities with least privilege.

findings.topic: Survey arXiv:2505.02279 taxonomy, comparison dimensions, and phased adoption roadmap
findings.summary: The survey (Ehtesham et al., v2 2025-05-23) positions the four protocols as interoperability tiers, each 'addressing interoperability in distinct deployment contexts': MCP = JSON-RPC client-server tool invocation/typed data exchange (Anthropic, Nov 2024); ACP = REST-native, MIME-typed multipart, session-aware messaging with RBAC/DID-integrated auth (IBM, Mar 2025); A2A = peer-to-peer task delegation with capability-based Agent Cards over HTTP+SSE (Google, Apr 2025); ANP = open-network decentralized discovery/collaboration via W3C DIDs + JSON-LD graphs. Comparison dimensions: 'interaction modes, discovery mechanisms, communication patterns, and security models' (abstract) and Table 7's axes: 'architectural choices, messaging formats, discovery methods, session models, and intended use cases' (§8). It traces lineage from KQML/FIPA-ACL speech-act performatives through SOA/ESB, RAG, and OpenAI function calling (2023), diagnosing static tool registries, ad-hoc security, and bespoke metadata as the gaps these protocols fix. Proposed roadmap (§9): Stage 1 MCP for tool invocation → Stage 2 ACP for cross-framework agent messaging → Stage 3 A2A for enterprise collaboration → Stage 4 ANP for 'decentralized agent marketplaces.' Per-protocol lifecycle threat tables (Tables 3-6) cover e.g. MCP 'tool poisoning, privilege persistence, and command injection... amplified by LLMs' susceptibility to prompt manipulation and opaque execution traces.'
findings.designImplication: The survey's tiering maps directly onto OS layering: Stage 1 (tool invocation) is the innermost ring — kernel IPC and capability enforcement; Stages 2-3 (agent messaging, task delegation) are OS services; Stage 4 (open-network discovery, marketplaces) is userspace daemons at the network edge. Its comparison axes (interaction mode, discovery, session model, security model) are the right requirement matrix for the OS IPC design doc. The threat analysis dictates kernel mitigations: tool-poisoning → signed/verified tool manifests at registration; privilege persistence → expiring, task-scoped capability grants; command injection → kernel-side schema validation of every tool call rather than trusting agent-side validation.

findings.topic: Synthesis: kernel-level vs userspace placement of protocol concepts in an agent-native OS
findings.summary: Across all four protocols the same concepts recur at different scopes, separating cleanly by invariance and trust requirements. Recur-everywhere and trust-critical (kernel candidates): correlated request/response + notification + cancellation framing (MCP JSON-RPC rules; A2A operations); capability declaration at handshake with mechanical enforcement (MCP capability negotiation, A2A AgentCard gating errors); durable task objects with a state machine including human/credential-blocked states (A2A 9-state lifecycle; MCP SEP-1686 converging to the same); session identity with resumable, ordered, replayable event streams (MCP Mcp-Session-Id + Last-Event-ID; A2A multi-stream broadcast ordering); principal identity and key custody (ANP did:wba, humanAuthorization, multi-DID); model-agnostic inference delegation (MCP sampling with 0-1 priority vectors and advisory hints); human-consent channels (MCP elicitation accept/decline/cancel; ANP humanAuthorization). Variable, evolving, and trust-tolerant (userspace candidates): discovery (A2A /.well-known/agent.json, ACP registries + package metadata + agent.yml, ANP /.well-known/agent-descriptions + search agents — three protocols, three mechanisms); protocol/modality negotiation (ANP natural-language meta-protocol with AI code generation; A2A modality negotiation); transport bindings beyond local IPC (A2A's three equivalent bindings; MCP Streamable HTTP; ACP REST); manifest authoring/publication/signing services; artifact rendering and marketplace/economic layers.
findings.designImplication: Kernel ships: (1) a canonical schema-defined message/task/capability ABI (a2a.proto-style single source of truth); (2) task objects with SUBMITTED/WORKING/INPUT_REQUIRED/AUTH_REQUIRED/REJECTED/terminal states as scheduler-native states; (3) capability-token passing on IPC channels (declare-then-enforce, undeclared → typed kernel error); (4) per-principal identity with derived task-scoped sub-identities and a human-consent gate on tagged operations; (5) the inference syscall with provider-abstract preference vectors; (6) replayable ordered event streams backed by the OS's persistent memory tier. Userspace ships (default-but-pluggable): discovery daemon (serving/crawling well-known manifests, registry sync), MCP/A2A/ACP transport bridges that adapt the kernel ABI to each wire protocol, the ANP-style negotiation service (LLM-driven, caching negotiated adapters via the self-improvement service), and card-signing/verification tooling above the kernel's raw verify primitive. This gives wire-protocol agnosticism: external MCP servers and A2A/ACP/ANP peers all terminate in userspace bridges that speak one kernel IPC dialect inward.

openQuestions: A2A v1.0 well-known discovery URI: the v1.0 spec page I fetched defines GetAgentCard/GetExtendedAgentCard operations but the discovery well-known path (/.well-known/agent.json in the 0.x era per the survey, reportedly /.well-known/agent-card.json since 0.3.0) lives in a separate 'Agent Discovery' guide — verify the exact normative URI before freezing the OS discovery daemon spec.

MCP tasks (SEP-1686) are explicitly 'experimental' in the 2025-11-25 revision — track whether the final task semantics converge with or diverge from A2A's 9-state lifecycle before committing the kernel task ABI to one shape.

Reported consolidation of ACP into A2A under the Linux Foundation (BeeAI/ACP merge announcements, mid-2025) was not verified from a primary source in this pass — if ACP is sunset, its unique contributions (offline package-embedded discovery, await/resume) may need to be adopted directly rather than via an ACP bridge.

A2A push notifications assume client-reachable HTTPS webhook endpoints; how should the OS map this to NAT'd/edge agents — kernel-managed webhook relay daemon, or translate webhooks into kernel event-stream subscriptions at the bridge?

The survey predates several relevant protocols (AGNTCY, Agora arXiv:2410.11905 'A Scalable Communication Protocol for Networks of Large Language Models' — cited by ANP as inspiration for meta-protocol negotiation, AP2/payments extensions); a follow-up pass should check whether any introduce kernel-relevant primitives not covered by MCP/A2A/ACP/ANP.

ANP's meta-protocol layer depends on AI code generation for negotiated protocol adapters — what sandboxing/verification does the OS require before executing negotiation-generated adapter code (relevant to the self-improvement service's trust model)?

Capability granularity mismatch: MCP negotiates feature-level capabilities, A2A declares skill-level capabilities, ANP describes service-level capabilities in JSON-LD — the kernel capability-token design needs one granularity (or a hierarchy) that can express all three without lossy translation in the bridges.
